title: [v5.8] Add migration code for BoltDB to SQLite
body:
This is gated behind a new option in `podman system migrate`, `--migrate-db`.

The basic logic is simple:
* Podman is already configured to use BoltDB
* Open a new, fresh SQLite database to write into
* Migrate all database contents as they exist in BoltDB, to SQLite. ** Do this as simply as possible: grab the object from the old DB
   and write it into the new DB using the standard Add and Save
   functions.
* Set the new database in the Runtime, close the old one.
* Move the old database file so it won't be reused
* Show a warning if the user explicitly configured BoltDB in containers.conf

Our ability to test complex migration scenarios is limited, but this should handle simple migrations easily.

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
Write `None` if there are no user-facing changes, otherwise enter your release note below.
Include "action required" if users need to take action when upgrading.
-->

```release-note
Added the ability to migrate from the legacy BoltDB database to SQLite via a new option to `podman system migrate`, `--migrate-db`.
```

This needs a Fixes: #27628
do we actually need the `--db` part? it would seem we already know the db and can detect and migrate it automatically.  I see the precedent but just curious.
also, on your last bullet, i think that should be a hard error before the migration ?
I'd prefer to require an explicit argument so we can error if they try and migrate with an existing SQLite DB

But the tmp path depends on the path returned after the db validation, so it is catch 22

We could just reload the DB unconditionally after successful acquisition of the alive lock? Opening the DB is a very cheap operation...

> We could just reload the DB unconditionally after successful acquisition of the alive lock? Opening the DB is a very cheap operation...

I guess that should be fine, it would also only need to happen here in 5.8 not for 6.

Maybe that then ties to another thing, the only time we know there are no parallel commands is during the initial refresh logic. If we are confident enough in this code we could perform it there unconditionally and have all users migrated without them knowing it.

The problem is `podman system service` - if that's active, we have another Libpod potentially altering state mid-migration. I don't think it's particularly unlikely, either, you'd just need an active service hitting our API on a regular enough basis to prevent auto-shutdown.

> The problem is `podman system service` - if that's active, we have another Libpod potentially altering state mid-migration. I don't think it's particularly unlikely, either, you'd just need an active service hitting our API on a regular enough basis to prevent auto-shutdown.

which is why I said refresh logic, that only happen once on the first podman command post boot. The service process would be no special there, if the service is the first process to start it will perform the refresh (+ then migration) and everything afterwards would take the new migrated thing.
Hmmm. Refresh logic makes sense, and would make things a lot more sensible... I'll give it some thought but I like the idea

Would make testing manually a pain, though. Only thing I can think of against it.

Unless we retain the option for manual migration, in case folks don't want to restart?

> Does running `podman system migrate --migrate-db` include everything that happens without the option or do we need to run both?

Reading the changes to `func (r *Runtime) Migrate(...)` makes me think that `--migrate-db` includes everything that happens without the option.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 00ca11536ae84656169fe45ca5e186de918af69d. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.

> I am interested in how the BoltDB to SQLite migration code is meant to be used from programs that wrap Podman, like Toolbx.
> 
> > > do we actually need the `--db` part? it would seem we already know the db and can detect and migrate it automatically. I see the precedent but just curious.
> > 
> > 
> > I'd prefer to require an explicit argument so we can error if they try and migrate with an existing SQLite DB
> 
> My initial thought was to agree with @baude about not needing the `--migrate-db` option because of the way Toolbx has been invoking `podman system migrate` for a while now. It's possible that I am getting this wrong, so here are some details.
> 
> Every time the `toolbox(1)` binary is invoked, it uses `podman version ...` to compare the current Podman version with the last seen version, stored in a stamp file, and runs `podman system migrate` if the current one is newer and updates the last seen version.
> 

I think we talked about this, you really should not be doing this. migrate is meant to be run without other podman commands in parallel generally (you cannot guarantee that), it stops all containers (toolbx shouldn't do that either), and it hasn't been needed since some very early 1.X days.


If we go down the auto refresh thing on boot nobody would need to do anything and we can just have the migrate option for manual testing.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 6fdd41b707277f96175118f56a8f78696bd33e6e. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Cockpit tests failed for commit 8bbdcb852069bbf661607a96bc5574d75d3ac006. @martinpitt, @jelly, @mvollmer please check.
Reworked to run automatically on refresh, as well as manually.
Unexpected testing problem: running on refresh is causing it to migrate the moment the bolt database is created. Maybe suppress this if CI_DESIRED_DATABASE is set? Will investigate further tomorrow.

You know, this really should not be working. We're syncing Pod state before creating containers, but pod state has the infra container's ID in it - which, in SQLite, is a foreign key on container ID. So SQLite should be refusing to commit our pod state update because the infra container ID refers to a container ID that doesn't exist (normally, the infra container ID only gets set after the infra container is created). Doesn't directly impact this PR but needs to be looked into.

Final remaining problem: some fields in inspect are non-deterministic in ordering. Namespaces, containers in pod inspect being most notable.

IMO, worth fixing in main, then I can cherry-pick into here.
[NON-BLOCKING] Packit jobs failed. @containers/packit-build please check. Everyone else, feel free to ignore.

@debarshiray The `--migrate-db` option does everything migrate normally does, yes, though it hasn't actually done anything since the Podman 1.0 days - it's been a long while since we had a DB change that required it. It should also exit cleanly if no migration was required or performed, though I think we print logs to that effect.

The current model will automatically migrate on reboot in most scenarios, so it may not be completely necessary to manually migrate, but it shouldn't hurt either. Biggest issue would be if we fail because the user hardcoded boltdb in the config file - there should be no data lost and no issues aside from the migration not being performed, but the command will fail.
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL, I think this is ready for review.

> > I am interested in how the BoltDB to SQLite migration code is meant to be used from programs that wrap Podman, like Toolbx.
> > > > do we actually need the `--db` part? it would seem we already know the db and can detect and migrate it automatically. I see the precedent but just curious.
> > > 
> > > I'd prefer to require an explicit argument so we can error if they try and migrate with an existing SQLite DB
> > 
> > My initial thought was to agree with @baude about not needing the `--migrate-db` option because of the way Toolbx has been invoking `podman system migrate` for a while now. It's possible that I am getting this wrong, so here are some details.
> > Every time the `toolbox(1)` binary is invoked, it uses `podman version ...` to compare the current Podman version with the last seen version, stored in a stamp file, and runs `podman system migrate` if the current one is newer and updates the last seen version.
> 
> I think we talked about this, you really should not be doing this. migrate is meant to be run without other podman commands in parallel generally (you cannot guarantee that), it stops all containers (toolbx shouldn't do that either), and it hasn't been needed since some very early 1.X days.

Yes, you are right.  I was about to delete the `podman system migrate` call from Toolbx when I read the Podman 6.0 plans to drop BoltDB, and paused to see how things evolve.  :)

> If we go down the auto refresh thing on boot nobody would need to do anything and we can just have the migrate option for manual testing.

Okay!  Is that the same thing @mheon mentioned [before](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/27660#issuecomment-3608747910).

> @debarshiray The `--migrate-db` option does everything migrate normally does, yes, though it hasn't actually done anything since the Podman 1.0 days - it's been a long while since we had a DB change that required it. It should also exit cleanly if no migration was required or performed, though I think we print logs to that effect.

Thanks for explaining that!

> The current model will automatically migrate on reboot in most scenarios, so it may not be completely necessary to manually migrate, but it shouldn't hurt either. Biggest issue would be if we fail because the user hardcoded boltdb in the config file - there should be no data lost and no issues aside from the migration not being performed, but the command will fail.

Okay!  That sounds like the same thing that @Luap99 said before, and your changes to `func (r *Runtime) refresh(...)` in   `libpod/runtime.go` seem to confirm that.

In that case, it seems better to remove the `podman system migrate` call from Toolbx.
Now targets the v5.8 branch. Ready for review.
CI Finally green
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL
